1: When Samuel grew old he appointed his sons as judges over Israel — Joel in Beersheba and Abijah in the south. The geographical posting to Beersheba, the southernmost major city of Judah, is notable: Samuel's sons are stationed far from Ramah, perhaps to extend judicial coverage to the periphery. The appointment of sons to succeed raises the specter of the Eli pattern: Eli's sons corrupted the Shiloh priesthood (1 Samuel 2:12-17); now Samuel's sons corrupt the southern judiciary. The parallel is the narrator's structural comment on institutional succession.
2: Joel and Abijah serve as judges at Beersheba. Joel means 'YHWH is God' and Abijah means 'my father is YHWH' — both names encode the covenant theology that their conduct will contradict. The irony of names meaning 'YHWH is God' and 'YHWH is my father' being given to men who perverted justice is the type of bitter wordplay the Deuteronomistic historian deploys throughout.
3: His sons did not walk in his ways but turned after dishonest gain (wayṭû ʾaḥărê haṯṯāʿ) — the word beṣaʿ (dishonest gain/unjust profit) appears in Exodus 18:21 as the negative quality Moses's judges must not have. Joel and Abijah took bribes and perverted justice. The perversion of justice by judicial officials was the central social-legal failure that the prophets would condemn throughout the monarchic period (Amos 5:12; Micah 3:11; Isaiah 1:23).
4: The elders of Israel gather and come to Samuel at Ramah. They constitute themselves as a formal delegation — this is a covenant assembly of representatives bringing a covenant complaint. The gathering at Ramah replicates the judicial geography of Samuel's circuit: they come to the judge's home city to address the judge directly. The complaint is legitimate: the judicial system Samuel established is failing at the succession level.
5: 'Behold, you are old and your sons do not walk in your ways. Now appoint for us a king to judge us like all the nations (kəḵol-haggôyim).' The three-part rationale — Samuel's age, sons' failure, comparison to the nations — is a formal constitutional petition. 'Like all the nations' is the theologically loaded phrase: Israel was constituted as explicitly not like the nations (Exodus 19:5-6, 'a kingdom of priests and a holy nation'). The request to be like the nations is a self-conscious rejection of Israel's covenant distinctiveness.
6: The thing displeased Samuel when they said 'Give us a king to judge us,' and Samuel prayed to YHWH. Samuel's displeasure is personal — he interprets the request as a rejection of his own leadership. YHWH immediately corrects this reading (v7): the rejection is not of Samuel but of YHWH. Samuel's initial displeasure, honestly recorded, shows that YHWH's prophets were not dispassionate instruments but real people with personal investments in their ministry.
7: YHWH says to Samuel: 'Obey the voice of the people in all that they say to you, for they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them.' YHWH's self-interpretation of Israel's request is the theological center of the chapter — and of the entire monarchy theology. The request for a human king is, at its root, a rejection of theocratic governance. YHWH is still the true king; the human king will be a concession to human weakness, not a superior arrangement.
8: 'According to all the deeds that they have done, from the day I brought them up from Egypt even to this day, forsaking me and serving other gods, so they are also doing to you.' YHWH places the demand for a king in the long history of Israelite apostasy: the Exodus → idolatry → judges cycle has been Israel's constant pattern. The monarchy is not a new departure but another iteration of the same basic rejection of YHWH's authority. The form changes; the problem is the same.
9: YHWH instructs Samuel to obey them but to 'solemnly warn them and show them the ways of the king who shall reign over them' (wəhāgêḏ tāgîḏ lāhem mišpaṭ hammelek ʾăšer yimlōḵ ʿălêhem). The phrase mišpaṭ hammelek — the 'manner/right/custom of the king' — describes the royal extraction system. Samuel is to make the political economy of monarchy visible before the people consent to it. The warning is educational disclosure, not merely complaint.
10: Samuel tells the people who are asking for a king all the words of YHWH. The speech that follows (vv11-18) is the most detailed description of royal administrative practice in the Hebrew Bible — and it accurately describes the administrative apparatus of a Near Eastern monarchy as evidenced from Ugarit, Egypt, and Mesopotamia.
11: 'This will be the behavior of the king who will reign over you: He will take your sons and appoint them to his chariots and to be his horsemen.' Chariot forces required specialized training and maintenance; royal chariot corps drew from the population by conscription. Israelite society had no standing chariot force — the demand for a king who 'fights our battles' (v20) implicitly required one. The taking of sons for military service is the first and most personally felt form of royal extraction.
12: The king will appoint commanders of thousands and commanders of fifties — a decimal military administrative structure consistent with both Israelite tribal organization and broader ANE armies. He will set some to plow and reap his ground — royal estates requiring labor levies — and to make weapons and equipment for his chariots. The combination of military and agricultural labor conscription was standard in both Egypt and Syria-Palestine for monarchic economies.
13: 'He will take your daughters to be perfumers and cooks and bakers.' Royal households required skilled domestic labor — perfumery (for cosmetics, anointing oils, and cultic use), cooking, and baking at a scale impossible for individual households. The Egyptian royal household records from the New Kingdom period list exactly these categories of female palace workers. Samuel's warning is not hypothetical; it describes what Israel could observe in Egyptian and Canaanite court economies.
14: 'He will take the best of your fields and vineyards and olive orchards and give them to his servants.' Royal land redistribution to courtiers and military officials was the economic engine of ANE monarchy — Ugaritic land grants to royal officials are extensively documented in the excavated archives. The taking of agricultural land was existentially threatening in a subsistence-farming economy where family land was both livelihood and inheritance.
15: 'He will take a tenth of your grain and your vineyards and give it to his officers and to his servants.' The ten-percent royal tithe is the tax structure documented in the Ugaritic economic texts and consistent with Egyptian administrative records. The irony is pointed: YHWH had required a tithe (Numbers 18:21-24; Deuteronomy 14:22-29) for the support of Levites and the poor; the king will require a parallel tithe for the support of his court apparatus.
16: 'He will take your male servants and female servants and the best of your young men and your donkeys and put them to his work.' The comprehensive extraction — human, animal, and agricultural labor — describes the corvée system (forced labor on royal projects) documented across the ancient Near East. Solomon's later use of corvée (1 Kings 5:13-18) and Rehoboam's threat to intensify it (1 Kings 12:4-14) are the historical fulfillments of this warning.
17: 'He will take a tenth of your flocks, and you shall be his slaves.' The closing intensification — from taking (vv11-16) to slave status — represents the endpoint of the royal extraction logic: a population sufficiently diminished by taxes, conscription, and land-seizure becomes functionally enslaved to the royal economy regardless of formal status. The word ʿăḇāḏîm (slaves/servants) is the term Israel used to describe their condition in Egypt.
18: 'And in that day you will cry out because of your king, whom you have chosen for yourselves, but YHWH will not answer you in that day.' The warning's culmination is that the very mechanism they are choosing — a king to 'fight our battles' — will produce the same kind of oppression that prompted Israel's original cry in Egypt. And unlike the Exodus, YHWH will not respond to this cry: they chose this, and the consequences are theirs to bear. The circular logic — from Egypt's slavery → Exodus → monarchy → new slavery — is the Deuteronomistic History's meta-narrative of Israel's freedom squandered.
19: The people refuse to listen to Samuel's voice: 'No! But there shall be a king over us.' The blunt refusal — lōʾ, no — followed by insistence is the covenant rejection in its baldest form. Samuel has delivered YHWH's word, described the consequences, and warned explicitly. The people have heard and chosen anyway. This is not ignorance but informed rejection.
20: 'That we also may be like all the nations, and that our king may judge us and go out before us and fight our battles.' The three functions of the desired king — judicial, military leadership, battle leadership — are all functions YHWH had been performing through judges and prophets. The explicit desire to 'be like all the nations' repeats the phrase of v5 and confirms the theological character of the request: Israel is choosing assimilation over covenant distinctiveness.
21: Samuel hears all the people's words and repeats them in the ears of YHWH. The act of speaking the people's request back to YHWH is intercession as reporting — Samuel presents the people's will to the divine king for final decision. YHWH has already decided (v7-9) but Samuel still acts as the proper mediatorial channel between the people and their sovereign.
22: YHWH says: 'Obey their voice and make them a king.' Samuel then dismisses the people to their own cities. YHWH's final word is concession without endorsement: 'make them a king' is compliance with their request, not approval of it. The concession theology — YHWH giving what is asked while warning of consequences — is the theological posture of a covenant partner who respects human freedom even when that freedom is used destructively. Samuel's dismissal sends the people home; the next chapter will show YHWH's specific choice of whom to anoint.
